paper_title: Ambrosia Pollen Back-Trajectory and Data Analysis

methods.description: This method calculates back-trajectories to identify possible source areas of Ambrosia pollen, using a methodology described by Stach et al. (2007). It helps in understanding the long-distance transport of pollen.

steps.description: Back-trajectories were calculated at the National Environmental Research Institute (NERI) in Denmark.
The back-trajectories were run at 6-hourly intervals throughout the study periods.
Each back-trajectory was run for 4 days with 6-hourly steps.

methods.description: This method involves collecting Ambrosia pollen data using volumetric spore traps and analyzing the samples through microscopy. It provides data on pollen concentrations over time.

steps.description: Ambrosia pollen data were collected at three sites in Poland using volumetric spore traps of the Hirst design.
steps.searchable_summary: collect pollen data using Hirst design spore traps

steps.description: The tape from the trap is divided into segments corresponding to 24 h periods and mounted for microscopy.
Pollen grains were counted using two different methods, requiring transformation of hourly counts into 2-hourly counts for comparison.
steps.searchable_summary: count pollen grains and transform data for comparison
